content.text: is cold: it does nothing until a collector subscribes. Writing the collector correctly determines whether the streaming experience is robust or fragile. This document covers collecting Claude's token deltas in a ViewModel, applying intermediate operators to transform and filter events before they reach the UI, handling errors without crashing the stream pipeline, and choosing the right coroutine scope so the job lifecycle matches the ViewModel — not the screen rotation.

attrs.alt: Flow processing pipeline: ClaudeRepository emits tokens → intermediate operators → ViewModel collector → MutableStateFlow → Compose UI; sealed class event types; scope comparison table
attrs.title: Flow Collectors for Claude Streaming Deltas

Choosing the wrong scope is one of the most common bugs in Android streaming integrations. The table below summarises the trade-offs for the three scopes you will encounter:
Sharing a Flow Between Multiple Collectors
restarts the network request each time a new collector subscribes. If two composables both want to observe the stream, do
multicast to any number of collectors with no duplicate requests:
pattern (one collector in the ViewModel writing to state) is almost always simpler than exposing
Without it, the user can send a second message before the first stream ends. Two concurrent collectors both write to
Collecting in lifecycleScope instead of viewModelScope.
The stream cancels on rotation. The user sees a half-completed response that never finishes, even if the underlying HTTP connection is still live.
Calling .collect on a cold Flow inside a composable.
Composables recompose and can start multiple collectors. Always collect in a ViewModel and expose
